title: Gradient Descent & Backpropagation
summary: The engine of all learning. Compute loss, compute gradients via chain rule (backprop), update parameters to reduce loss. Gradient flow through the architecture determines trainability — this is why residuals, LayerNorm, and scaling all exist.
key_insights: Gradient = direction of steepest increase. We go opposite: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla L$
Backprop = reverse-mode autodiff. Compute ALL gradients in ONE backward pass — cost ≈ 2× forward

Chain rule through depth: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial h_N} \cdots \frac{\partial h_2}{\partial h_1} \cdot \frac{\partial h_1}{\partial \theta_1}$. Product of many terms → vanishing or exploding
Vanishing gradients killed deep networks before residuals. $\prod_i \|J_i\| < 1$ each → product $\to 0$ exponentially
Every architectural trick (residuals, LayerNorm, $1/\sqrt{d_k}$, Xavier init) exists to keep gradients in a healthy range
Learning rate $\eta$ is the most important hyperparameter. Too high = diverge, too low = stuck

mathematical_core.chain_rule: $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial h} \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta}$$ applied recursively through layers
mathematical_core.backprop_cost: Forward: $O(P)$ FLOPs where $P$ = number of params. Backward: $\approx 2 \times O(P)$. Total training step: $\approx 3 \times$ forward.

derivations.title: Why gradients vanish in deep networks

steps.name: Chain rule through $N$ layers
steps.math: $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_1} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} J_i \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_N}$$ where $J_i = \frac{\partial h_{i+1}}{\partial h_i}$ is the Jacobian of layer $i$.

steps.name: Sigmoid example
steps.math: $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$, max value $= 0.25$ at $x=0$. Through $N$ layers: $\|\text{gradient}\| \leq 0.25^N$. At $N=10$: $\approx 10^{-6}$.

steps.name: ReLU partially fixes this
steps.math: $\text{ReLU}'(x) = 1$ for $x > 0$, $0$ for $x < 0$. Gradient is either 1 (preserved) or 0 (dead). No shrinkage, but dead neuron problem.

steps.name: Residuals fully fix this
steps.math: $J_{\text{residual}} = I + J_{\text{sublayer}}$. Product always includes $I$ as leading term. Gradient cannot vanish completely.

derivations.consequence: Vanishing gradients → sigmoid replaced by ReLU (2012) → residuals enabled very deep networks (2015) → transformers (2017) combine residuals + LN + scaling.

derivations.title: Learning rate and loss landscape geometry

steps.name: Second-order view
steps.math: $$L(\theta + \delta) \approx L(\theta) + \nabla L^\top \delta + \frac{1}{2} \delta^\top H \delta$$ where $H$ is the Hessian. Optimal step: $\delta^* = -H^{-1} \nabla L$ (Newton's method).

steps.name: Why we don't use Newton's
steps.math: $H \in \mathbb{R}^{P \times P}$. For GPT-3 ($P = 175B$): $H$ has $3 \times 10^{22}$ entries. Impossible to compute or store.

steps.name: Adam approximation
steps.math: Adam tracks running mean ($m$) and variance ($v$) of gradients. Update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot m_t / (\sqrt{v_t} + \epsilon)$. Diagonal approximation of $H^{-1} \nabla L$.

derivations.consequence: Adam = cheap approximation to Newton's method. Works well in practice because loss landscapes have structure exploitable by diagonal preconditioning.

geometric_properties: Loss landscape is high-dimensional and non-convex, but has exploitable structure (connected low-loss valleys)
Gradient direction is the best first-order move. Curvature (Hessian) tells you how far to go.
Saddle points are more common than local minima in high dimensions — gradient descent escapes them naturally
statistical_properties: Gradient norm $\|\nabla L\|$ should decrease during training (approaching optimum)
Monitor gradient norm per layer — large discrepancies indicate vanishing/exploding
Gradient noise (from mini-batches) acts as regularizer — see FOUNDATIONS.BATCHING_SGD
numerical_properties: float16 gradients: values $< 6 \times 10^{-8}$ underflow to zero. Loss scaling compensates.
Gradient accumulation: sum gradients over $K$ mini-batches before updating. Effective batch size = $K \times B$.
Gradient clipping: if $\|\nabla L\| > \text{max\_norm}$, scale down. Prevents exploding gradients in RNNs/early transformer training.
notes: Autograd internals are engine-level, not needed for architecture innovation. Conceptual understanding of gradient flow IS needed for designing trainable architectures.
